plainMeaning: I bow with all my heart to Lord Ajita, who has conquered every enemy and every fear; may he wash away my wrongdoing.
recitation: ajiaṁ jiārigaṇaṁ, jia savvabhayaṁ bhavohariu | paṇamāmi ahaṁ payao, pāvaṁ pasameu me bhayavaṁ ||

wordByWord.gloss: Ajita (the Unconquered), the 2nd Tīrthaṅkara — accusative, object of homage
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. *ajita* (a- 'not' + jita 'conquered'); Prakrit *ajia*, acc. *ajiaṁ*. The name itself means 'never vanquished.'
wordByWord.notes: Bivalent name-play with the verb jia/jita that follows: the 'Unconquered' is praised as the conqueror.

wordByWord.gloss: one who has conquered the host of foes (acc.)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. *jita-ari-gaṇa* (jita 'conquered' + ari 'enemy' + gaṇa 'host, troop'); Prakrit acc. *jiārigaṇaṁ*.
wordByWord.notes: The ari-gaṇa is read doctrinally as the inner enemies — the kaṣāyas (passions) — not literal armies.
Heads the next epithet, joining with savvabhayaṁ: 'who has conquered all fear.'
Echoes the hymn's abhaya (fearlessness) motif; cf. the sevenfold bhaya of Jain lists.

wordByWord.gloss: remover of the flood of worldly existence (acc.)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. *bhava-ogha-hara* (bhava 'rebirth, existence' + ogha 'flood, torrent' + hara 'remover'); Prakrit *bhavoha-hariu* with vowel-sandhi bhava+ogha → bhavoha.
wordByWord.notes: Saṁsāra figured as an overwhelming current the Jina sweeps away.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. *praṇamāmi* (pra + √nam 'to bend, bow'), 1st sg. present; Prakrit *paṇamāmi*.

wordByWord.notes: Explicit 'I' underscores the personal, first-person turn of the verse.

wordByWord.gloss: reverently, with composed and self-restrained devotion
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. *prayata* 'self-controlled, intent, ritually composed' (pra + √yam 'to restrain'); Prakrit *payao*, here adverbial/predicative to the speaker.
wordByWord.notes: Marks the bow as bhāva-namaskāra — done with a disciplined, attentive mind, not mechanically.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. *praśamayatu* / *praśāmyatu* (pra + √śam 'to be pacified, cease'), 3rd sg. imperative; Prakrit *pasameu*.
wordByWord.notes: Benedictive petition — 'let (the Lord) still my sin.'

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. *me*, enclitic gen./dat. of *aham*; same in Prakrit.
Skt. *bhagavān* 'possessing bhaga — fortune, majesty, holiness'; Prakrit *bhayavaṁ*, nom. sg.
wordByWord.notes: Grammatical subject of pasameu: it is the Lord who is asked to quell the sin.

literalTranslation: Ajita, conqueror of the host of foes, who has conquered all fear, remover of the flood of existence — to him I, reverent and composed, bow; may the Lord quell my sin.
idiomaticTranslation: To Ajita — who has vanquished the whole army of inner foes, who has overcome every fear, who sweeps away the torrent of rebirth — I bow with disciplined devotion; may the Blessed One still all my sin.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the royal-sovereign portrait of the preceding stanza, the hymn now swings back to Ajita, and the poet packs the line with a tight cluster of ji-/jia- epithets that pivot on the Jina's very name. The paradox is deliberate: a-jita, 'the never-conquered,' is hymned precisely as the supreme conqueror — jiārigaṇaṁ (vanquisher of the foe-host), jia savvabhayaṁ (vanquisher of all fear). Read against Jain doctrine, the 'host of enemies' is not an outward army but the inner adversaries — the kaṣāyas (anger, pride, deceit, greed) and the passions that bind the soul; to be Jina is exactly to have won that interior war. bhavohariu then widens the frame: he is the one who carries off the very flood (ogha) of saṁsāra. With these three accusative epithets the Jina is fully characterized as object of worship; the verse then turns on its hinge to the worshipper. paṇamāmi ahaṁ payao foregrounds the first-person act of bowing, and the single word payao (Skt. prayata) is the weight-bearing term: the prostration is to be made with a composed, self-restrained, ritually intent mind — bhāva-namaskāra rather than a hollow gesture. The closing petition, pāvaṁ pasameu me bhayavaṁ, completes the movement from praise to plea: having bowed, the reciter asks the Lord to pacify his own sin, the line landing on bhayavaṁ, 'the Blessed One,' as the agent who can do so.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse compresses three doctrinal points. First, the etymological theology of 'Jina': spiritual victory is interior — the conquest of kaṣāya and the eighteen pāpa-sthānaka — so jiārigaṇaṁ glosses what it means to be a Tīrthaṅkara. Second, abhaya: 'who has conquered all fear' presents the Jina as the fearless one and, by extension, the giver of fearlessness to devotees. Third, the soteriology of crossing: bhava-ogha figures rebirth as a flood, and the Jina as the ford-maker who removes it. The devotee's side is equally doctrinal: payao signals that homage is efficacious only as bhāva (inner intent), and pāvaṁ pasameu frames the pacification of sin as the fruit of such devotion — the same confessional logic that animates pratikramaṇa.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, bow with the mind the word payao demands — composed, attentive, restrained — not mechanically. Let 'conqueror of the foe-host' redirect the prayer inward: the enemies to be defeated are one's own passions, here and now. Take the closing line as a genuine personal petition for the quieting of one's faults, in keeping with the self-examination of the rite.

crossReferences.text: Sarva-bhaya / abhaya — the Jina as abhaya-dātā (giver of fearlessness)

notes: ભવોહરિઉ is parsed bhava-ogha-hariu (acc. of bhava-ogha-hara/-hariya, 'remover of the flood of existence') via the regular sandhi bhava+ogha → bhavoha; the book's reading is preserved verbatim. પયઓ (payao) is taken as Skt. prayata, 'composed/self-restrained/intent,' construed adverbially with the bowing speaker; some readers gloss it 'devoutly/with reverence' — the senses converge. The standalone જિઅ heads the compound jia-savvabhayaṁ ('conqueror of all fear'). The verse turns on the deliberate Ajita/jita word-play. Some recensions of this stanza read the second epithet as 'bhavohariuṁ ca' or vary the daṇḍa placement; the Anchalgachchha book's punctuation and orthography are followed here. Doctrinal reading of ari-gaṇa as the inner passions is the standard Jain interpretation, not an explicit gloss in the verse itself.
